Question it leaves open: Why is damage not repaired when repair capacity exists (embryo, germline)?
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Damage is one input; the failure is the loop defending a drifting reference, so it is not corrected

Its core claim: Programs good early are harmful late
Question it leaves open: Why do the SAME programs persist unchanged?
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Directional reference drift (b>0) is the hyperfunction term; the loop cannot sense it is now maladaptive

Its core claim: Limited resource → under-invest in somatic maintenance
Question it leaves open: Why does investment fail even when resources abundant?
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Soma lacks an external reference to invest toward; germline keeps one

Question it leaves open: What functional quantity is lost, and why is loss directional?
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Names the lost quantity precisely: the calibration of set-points to the true optimum (observability)

Its core claim: Recovery slows, variance rises near a tipping point
Question it leaves open: What mechanism causes the basin to flatten?
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Controller gain built from the drifting substrate → gain decays as references decorrelate

Its core claim: Sum of deleterious age-changes reduces fitness
Question it leaves open: Which changes are causal vs bystander?
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Causal subset = changes to stored references; bystanders = changes the loop can still correct

Its core claim: Cell competition → either cancer or senescence
How Reference-Decay unifies / extends it: Competition needs a fitness reference; drift of that reference is the deeper unobservable
